pending.body:

Chris Gaertner on our team has spent time around insurance (iLife, and his family has been at State Farm for years), so this is a space we follow closely.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.body:

One more note. I think the APImetrics acquisition was a smart move, combining monitoring with conformance and security into one platform. As companies ship more API-first products, having a single place to catch drift and performance issues before customers do becomes increasingly important. Already working with RingCentral and partnering with Akamai is great early validation.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.subject: Early Growth Investors | Telescope Partners
pending.body:
Hey Salman,

Last note on Astrada. I think the "bring your own card" approach is a really clean way to solve the visibility gap. Most expense platforms only see spend on cards they issue, which means almost half of what a company actually spends is invisible to them. Plugging directly into the card networks and delivering structured data at authorization gives platforms something they've never had before. $750M in card spend processed since launch is great early traction.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.subject: saw Bixby keeps coming up | quick intro Telescope
pending.body:

One more thought on Bixby. I think the copilot approach to submittal review makes a lot of sense for construction. Having AI cross-reference hundreds of pages and flag non-compliant products while keeping humans in the decision seat feels like the right balance for an industry where mistakes are expensive and trust in new tools is earned slowly.

Our Head of Ops Harrison Doyle is a former Procore VP of Finance, so construction workflows are something we spend a lot of time on.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.subject: AI governance and compliance | Telescope intro
pending.body:
Hey Dilip,

Wanted to share one more thought on CognitiveView. With the EU AI Act high-risk enforcement deadline coming in August, I think the shadow AI detection piece is especially timely. Most companies I talk to don't even have a full inventory of what AI tools their teams are using, and that's exactly what regulators are going to ask for first. Helping companies get that visibility before enforcement kicks in feels like a natural entry point.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.subject: Telescope Partners <> ContractorCommerce

Letting homeowners get quotes and book services outside business hours solves a real problem for both sides.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.subject: Telescope Partners <> ControlTheory
pending.body:
Hey Bob,

Last note on ControlTheory. I think the timing on Dstl8 is really interesting. As teams ship more AI-generated code, the gap between how fast code gets written and how well anyone understands what it's doing at runtime is only growing. Analyzing telemetry at the edge instead of collecting everything and sorting through it later is a much better fit for how development actually works now. The StackEngine and CopperEgg exits give me confidence the team knows how to build and scale observability products.

Chris Gaertner on our team previously worked closely with Datadog, so infrastructure observability is a space we follow.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.subject: Telescope Partners <> Corgea Intro

Most teams I talk to have thousands of findings sitting in a backlog because nobody has time to write the patches. The remediation-first angle is what makes this interesting to us. Chris has worked closely with companies like Axonius and JumpCloud, so security infrastructure is a space we know well.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.subject: Telescope Partners <> Corvera Intro
pending.body:
Hey Chris, last note on Corvera. The $0 to $33k MRR in four weeks stat stuck with me. I think the reason CPG brands haven't automated their back office yet is that the tools were always built for enterprise supply chains, not for a brand doing $5M in revenue that still processes orders out of their inbox. Going from email to invoice to fulfillment without anyone touching it is a real unlock for that segment.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.subject: Telescope <> Cotool Intro
pending.body:
Hey Max, one more thought on Cotool. I think the move from one-off investigations to always-on agents with a single click is really smart. Every SOC team I've heard from has the same problem: they build a runbook for something, then it sits in a wiki and nobody uses it. Turning investigations into reusable automations solves that in a way that actually sticks. Chris has worked closely with Axonius and JumpCloud, so security is a space we've spent a lot of time in.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.subject: Telescope Partners <> DiversiFi Intro
pending.body:
Hey Caleb, one more thought on DiversiFi. I think the 3PL billing problem is one of those things that's way more broken than people outside the industry realize. Every shipment has a different rate, a different carrier markup, a different set of surcharges, and most 3PLs are still reconciling all of that manually or in spreadsheets. 20-25% revenue increases with early customers tells me a lot of that margin was just leaking through the cracks. Chris has worked with companies like Project44 and Parabola, so logistics and supply chain is a space we know well.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.subject: Telescope Partners <> Entangl Intro
pending.body:
Hey Shapol, last thought on Entangl. I think the insight that design errors in data centers get caught way too late is a big one. Engineers spend roughly two months a year just on reviews, and projects still slip by seven months on average because problems get found after the fact instead of when they're introduced. Continuous monitoring across all the interconnected systems feels like the only way that actually scales given how fast these facilities are being built right now.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.subject: Telescope Partners <> Faction Intro
pending.body:
Hey Kunal, one more note on Faction. I was looking at the case study where a $1B manufacturer eliminated their quote backlog and increased win rates after deploying in four weeks. I think the real unlock is that distributors lose deals not because of price but because they're too slow to get a quote back, and that's entirely a data entry and product matching problem. 95%+ accuracy on product matching from messy PDFs and emails is impressive. Chris has worked with companies like Parabola and Paperless Parts, so distribution and manufacturing is a space we've spent real time in.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.subject: Telescope Partners <> Fakto Intro
pending.body:
Hey Nicolas, last thought on Fakto. The stat that enterprises lose 2-5% of supplier spend to billing errors and underutilized contract terms really resonated with me. I think the reason nobody catches it is that reconciling millions of invoice lines against thousands of contracts manually just doesn't happen at the frequency it needs to. Moving from periodic audits to continuous, line-by-line reconciliation feels like the only approach that actually captures the full value.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.subject: Telescope <> Flowgen Intro | do you know Ariadna?

I think the reason most AI-in-ERP efforts stall is that they try to replace the system instead of making the existing one run autonomously. Being live across SAP, NetSuite, and Dynamics suggests the approach translates across environments, which is a hard thing to do.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.body: Hey Jeff, circling back on this. We're flexible on timing so LMK if a different week works better for coffee or a quick Zoom.

pending.subject: Coffee in LA next week? | Telescope Partners Intro
pending.body:
Jeff,

Congrats on the stealth launch and the Auctus acquisition. I think acquiring an existing RCM services business to immediately add $2M ARR and 300 plastic surgery and derm providers is a smart way to prove the technology works on real volume before scaling it. The combination of a software founder, a practicing surgeon, and an engineer as co-founders is also exactly the kind of team that tends to win in a space where domain credibility is table stakes. We've spent time around healthcare services through Knowtion Health and Passage Health and I'd love to learn more.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.subject: Coffee in LA next week? | Telescope Partners
pending.body: Hey Brent, circling back on this. We're flexible on timing so LMK if a different week works better for coffee or a quick Zoom.

pending.subject: Coffee in LA next week? | Telescope Partners
pending.body:
Brent,

I was reading about Walkway's positioning as the pricing intelligence layer for experience operators and I think the Airbnb angle is genuinely useful context here because dynamic pricing for vacation rentals went from exotic to expected, and I'd imagine you're betting the same thing happens in tours and activities over the next few years. The $1.65M pre-seed from Reformation Partners suggests travel-focused investors see the same timing.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.subject: Telescope Partners <> Clipp
pending.body: Hey Albert, was talking to a restaurant operator recently who mentioned they have three different systems for invoices, payroll, and accounting and none of them talk to each other. Curious how you're approaching the integration challenge.

pending.subject: Telescope Partners <> Clipp
pending.body:
Albert,

I was looking at how Clipp operates as a two-sided platform, giving suppliers an ops and payment layer while giving restaurant buyers a mobile app for 24/7 ordering. I think the two-sided dynamic is actually the harder and more interesting problem to solve in food distribution because it's not enough to automate the supplier side if the buyers are still calling in orders. Being built by people who ran restaurants and food supply businesses tends to matter when you're trying to get operators to change how they actually buy.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.subject: Telescope Partners <> Pesto Inro
pending.body: Hey Kate, your GrowUp Farms background is really interesting. Curious how that experience shapes the way you think about building for operators who are juggling a million things at once.

pending.subject: Telescope Partners <> Pesto Inro
pending.body:
Kate,

I think the positioning of Pesto as an AI-first ERP purpose-built for food and beverage SMEs is interesting because most of the existing options are either generic ERP systems that don't understand catch weights and perishables, or point solutions that don't talk to each other. The $1.1M raise from Fuel Ventures and the plan to expand into North America in 2026 is well-timed given how much more willing F&B operators have gotten to adopt software in the last couple of years.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.subject: B2B commerce for wholesalers | Telescope intro
pending.body: Hey Uri, was talking to a wholesale distributor recently who mentioned that their B2B buyers now expect the same experience they get shopping online. Curious how you're thinking about the self-service vs. rep-assisted model.

pending.subject: B2B commerce for wholesalers | Telescope intro
pending.body:
Uri,

I was looking at the mobile app with barcode scanning and offline functionality and I think that's the piece most wholesale platforms miss because a lot of the actual ordering in distribution still happens in the field, not in front of a desktop. The quote-to-cart flow that avoids manual copy-paste is also genuinely useful for the rep side of the house. Chris on our team worked with PartsTech and ShopGenie so we've spent time in vertical B2B commerce.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.subject: Telescope <> Coolr Reconnect
pending.body: Hey Durlabh, I was looking at the VistaZ camera setup and I think the retrofit approach is smart. No wires, no plugs, just peel and stick inside an existing cooler. That removes the biggest barrier to getting deployed at scale. I'd be curious how the conversation changes once brands like Heineken or Carlsberg start seeing the sell-out data. Does it shift from "we need better availability" to "we want to use this for assortment planning and shelf optimization"?

pending.subject: Telescope <> Coolr Reconnect
pending.body:
Durlabh,

I think the decision to position Coolr's value around the analytics layer rather than the hardware is the right call because it means you're not stuck in a hardware refresh cycle and you can work with whatever cameras brands already have installed. Heineken and Carlsberg and Freshpet are meaningful early logos given how operationally conservative those organizations tend to be about new vendor relationships. I'd be curious how you're thinking about expanding the playbook beyond cooler compliance into broader in-store execution for food and bev brands.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.subject: Telescope Partners <> Nexcade Intro
pending.body: Hey Dan, I read the FreightWaves piece about Nexcade and the insight that forwarders were taking automated data and putting it back into spreadsheets really stood out. I think that's the right problem to build around. Chris has worked with companies like Parabola and Project44 so we spend a lot of time in supply chain. Curious whether you're seeing more pull from mid-market forwarders or whether the larger players like XPO are driving adoption.

pending.subject: Telescope Partners <> Nexcade Intro
pending.body:
Dan,

I was looking at how Nexcade is specifically targeting the exception-heavy edge cases like hazmat and out-of-gauge cargo that most RPA-style automation breaks on. I think that's actually the right wedge into freight forwarding because the easy workflows are already automated and the 3PLs that are still doing manual work are dealing with exactly those edge cases. Chris on our team worked with Project44 and Parabola and we spend a lot of time in the logistics stack.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.subject: Telescope <> Freshline Intro
pending.body: Hey Robert, I was looking at the AI ordering feature where customers can just text something like "50 lbs Atlantic salmon for Friday" and the system handles the catch-weight pricing and confirmation automatically. That's a clever way to digitize ordering without forcing a behavior change. Curious whether most of your 100+ distributors started with the AI ordering piece or the storefront, and how the adoption path typically plays out.

pending.subject: Telescope <> Freshline Intro
pending.body:
Robert,

I was looking at how Freshline handles catch weight pricing and complex delivery cutoffs natively rather than patching around them, which I think is what separates a real food distribution platform from a generic B2B eCommerce tool that happens to sell to distributors. 100+ food distributors and a 98% collection rate are both numbers that matter a lot to a buyer evaluating whether this actually works in production. Chris on our team worked with Parabola and Project44 and we've spent time in the food supply chain.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.subject: Telescope Partners <> Reform
pending.body: Hey Omar, I was looking at the customer numbers on your site, 20M+ tasks automated and 250K+ hours saved is serious traction. The drag-and-drop workflow builder approach makes sense for logistics ops teams that need to move fast without waiting on engineering. Chris has worked with companies like Parabola and Project44 so we have good context on how supply chain companies adopt new tools. Curious whether most customers start with document extraction and expand from there, or if they come in for a specific workflow like AP or customs declarations.

pending.subject: Telescope Partners <> Reform
pending.body:
Omar,

I was looking at how Reform is tackling customs declaration prep and duty drawback alongside the standard freight forwarding workflows. I think those are the processes that tend to be completely invisible to most automation vendors because they require regulatory knowledge, not just data entry automation, and the error cost is disproportionate. $500M+ in AP invoices processed and 20M+ tasks automated is a real number at this stage. Chris on our team worked with Project44 and Parabola and knows the logistics software stack well.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.

pending.subject: Telescope <> Peasy Intro | CPG Deep Dive
pending.body: Hey Ryan, I saw the Shelf Engine background for you and Bryan, and I think that experience gives you a real edge in understanding how CPG operators actually manage inventory day to day. The free tier is an interesting GTM decision. Curious whether that's driving organic adoption or if most brands are finding you through the Shopify/QuickBooks ecosystem.

pending.subject: Telescope <> Peasy Intro | CPG Deep Dive
pending.body:
Ryan,

I was looking at how Peasy handles the full purchasing-to-production loop with automatic lot code generation and landed cost tracking, rather than just being another inventory counter for CPG brands. I think the free pricing model is an aggressive way to build distribution quickly in a market where the incumbent tools charge a lot for functionality that scrappy brands don't need. The CPG operating background from running an actual brand tends to show up in the product decisions in ways that generic software doesn't replicate easily.

If now isn't the right time, totally understand. But would love to connect whenever there's a window.
